title: Mandarin Chinese — Beginner
description: Complete beginner course for Mandarin Chinese with 12 chapters covering greetings, numbers, food, travel, and more.

phrase.breakdown: Literal: 'You good'. The most common greeting.
Similar but with a question particle.

prompt.breakdown: Said when going to bed or parting late at night.

phrase.breakdown: Adding 'ma' turns a statement into a yes/no question.
Standard positive response to 'How are you?'
Polite way to say you're not doing well.
Used to return the same question to the speaker.
Literally 'horse horse tiger tiger' - means mediocre or just okay.
Most common way to express gratitude.
Literal: 'Don't be polite'. Standard response to thanks.
Polite request marker, used before requests.
Asking if someone is able to do something.
Literal: 'No relation/matter'. Response to apology or thanks.

prompt.breakdown: Softer apology or way to get attention, like 'excuse me'.

phrase.breakdown: Standard way to introduce yourself by name.

prompt.breakdown: Literal: 'Know you very happy'. Means 'Nice to meet you'.

phrase.breakdown: Asking which country someone is from.
Asking what country this place is.
Can mean 'yes' or used as 'to be' verb.
Affirmative response meaning something is correct.
Agreement or acknowledgment of a request.

prompt.breakdown: Negative form of 'to be', means 'is not' or 'no'.

phrase.breakdown: Basic question word asking for information about things.
Question word asking for reasons or explanations.
Used to give reasons, answer to 'why'.
Connects cause and effect, means 'so' or 'therefore'.
Question word asking about method or manner.
I confirm I am a student.

prompt.breakdown: Question asking if you are a teacher.

phrase.breakdown: The number 2. Use 'èr' when counting or in phone numbers.
The number 3. Three horizontal strokes representing the quantity.
The number 4. Be careful with pronunciation - sounds similar to 'death' (死) in some dialects.

prompt.breakdown: The number 5, middle of the first ten numbers.

phrase.breakdown: The number 6. Sounds like 'smooth' (流) - considered lucky.
The number 7. Important for dates and counting.
Lucky number in Chinese culture, sounds like 'prosperity' (发).
The number 10, foundation for larger numbers in Chinese.

prompt.breakdown: The number 0, used in phone numbers, decimals, and as a placeholder.

phrase.breakdown: Literal: 'Two-ten'. Tens are formed by number + 十.
Literal: 'Three-ten'. Pattern continues for all tens.
Often just said as '百' when it's exactly 100.
Literal: 'Week-one'. Days use numbers 1-6, then 天 or 日 for Sunday.
Literal: 'Week-two'. Second day of the week.
Literal: 'Week-four'. Fourth day of the week.
Also can say 星期日 (xīngqī rì). Both mean Sunday.
Common question to ask what day of the week it is.
Important: Use 两 (liǎng), not 二 (èr), when saying 2 o'clock.

prompt.breakdown: Standard way to ask 'What time is it now?'

phrase.breakdown: 半 (bàn) means 'half', used for 30 minutes past the hour.
分 (fēn) means 'minute'. Pattern: hour + 点 + minute + 分.

prompt.breakdown: 刻 (kè) means 'quarter hour'. 一刻 = 15 minutes.
Literal: 'Lack 10 minutes (to) 9 o'clock' = 8:50. Alternative way to express 'to the hour'.

phrase.breakdown: Literal: 'First month'. Months use numbers 1-12 + 月.
Pattern: month + 月 + day + 号. Can also use 日 (rì) instead of 号 in formal contexts.
Common way to ask what date (day of month) it is.
The day after today. 明 originally means 'bright/clear'.
上个星期 (shàng ge xīngqī) means 'last week'.

prompt.breakdown: Means 'this month'. 下个月 = next month, 上个月 = last month.

phrase.breakdown: Literal: 'Current year'. 明年 = next year, 去年 = last year.

options.translation: I go to school at 8 o'clock
I go to school at 9 o'clock
He goes to school at 8 o'clock

phrase.breakdown: Use 两 not 二 for 2 o'clock.

prompt.breakdown: How to go / How to get there?

phrase.breakdown: How to go / How to get there?

scenarios.title: Ordering Street Food
scenarios.description: You are exploring a bustling night market and smell delicious dumplings.

phrasebook.nativeScript: 请问，有什么馅儿的饺子？
phrasebook.romanization: Qǐngwèn, yǒu shénme xiànr de jiǎozi?
phrasebook.translation: Excuse me, what fillings do the dumplings have?

phrasebook.romanization: Wǒ xiǎng yào yí fèn jiǎozi.
phrasebook.translation: I'd like one portion of dumplings.
I want pork and cabbage filling.
How much is it in total?
Can it be a little cheaper?

scenarios.title: Checking into a Hotel
scenarios.description: You have just arrived at your hotel after a long flight.

scenarios.title: Shopping for Souvenirs
scenarios.description: You are in a local gift shop looking for a present.

scenarios.title: Asking for Directions
scenarios.description: You are lost in the city center.
